title: Moduli spaces with abelian motives
speaker: Victoria Hoskins
abstract: I will explain how several different moduli spaces of bundles on a smooth projective curve have abelian motives. Our starting point is a formula for the motive of the stack of vector bundles on the curve in Voevodsky's category of motives with rational coefficients. I will then explain how conservatively properties for abelian motives can be harnessed to obtain motivic formulae and provide motivic lifts of known cohomological phenomena, such as chi-independence and mirror symmetry. This is joint work with Simon Pepin Lehalleur.

proof_stages.label: Tautological classes suggest curve-generated motives
proof_stages.summary: Starting from a moduli space with a universal bundle, tautological generation of cohomology motivates the expectation that its motive should be generated by the motive of the curve, hence should be abelian and accessible via conservativity.

proof_stages.label: Bundle, Higgs, SL/PGL moduli spaces
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes a smooth projective curve and rank-degree data, defines stacks and moduli spaces of vector bundles and Higgs bundles, explains the Hitchin map and scaling action, and records the fixed-determinant and quotient versions for SL_n and PGL_n.

proof_stages.label: Cohomological blueprint and obstructions
proof_stages.summary: Known rational cohomology results distinguish tautologically generated cases, degree dependence for vector bundles, degree independence for Higgs bundles, and the nontrivial gamma-variant part for SL Higgs moduli, setting the targets and limitations for motivic analogues.

proof_stages.label: Motivic toolkit and abelian subcategories
proof_stages.summary: Voevodsky motives, realization functors, Chow groups as Hom groups, and the subcategories generated by M(C) or by abelian varieties are introduced, with Wildeshaus conservativity singled out as the mechanism for later motivic liftings.

proof_stages.label: Bun motive formula from flag Quot approximations
proof_stages.summary: Assuming a rational point on the curve, the motive of Bun_{n,d} is computed as a product involving M(BG_m), M(Jac(C)), and motivic zeta factors by approximating Bun with Quot schemes, resolving them by flag Quot schemes that are iterated projective bundles, applying small-map invariant arguments, and passing to a symmetric-algebra homotopy colimit.

proof_stages.label: Higgs motives via fixed loci and chain wall-crossing
proof_stages.summary: The semiprojective G_m-action reduces Higgs motives to fixed components, the nonzero fixed components are moduli of chains, and wall-crossing plus Hecke-type computations show that GL Higgs motives are generated by C while SL Higgs motives remain abelian after adjoining finite covers of C.

proof_stages.label: Conservativity applications
proof_stages.summary: Because the relevant motives are abelian or geometric-abelian, conservativity lifts cohomological identities to motivic statements, including low-rank formulas, the GL = SL times Jacobian decomposition, degree independence for Higgs moduli, and motivic SL/PGL mirror symmetry.

examples.name: Betti/character moduli comparison
examples.note: The Betti moduli space of twisted surface-group representations is mentioned as an optional nonabelian-Hodge comparison explaining the known cohomological degree-independence for Higgs moduli, not as part of the motivic proof.

examples.name: Rank-one degree-zero bundle stack without a rational point
examples.note: In response to a question, the stack Bun_{1,0} is used to show why the rational-point hypothesis matters: without a rational point the relevant G_m-gerbe over the Jacobian need not split, so the product motive formula is not automatic.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker uses Bun_{1,0} to analyze the rational-point hypothesis, identifying it as a G_m-torsor over the Jacobian and tracing how triviality affects the expected motive formula
